findings.title: Reset can race with the in-flight turn and emit stale error/completion output after `/new`
findings.description: This new early-return path kills the session and deletes all per-thread state immediately, but it does not coordinate with any already-running `process_turn_events` for the current turn. When that older event loop later sees its `event_rx` close, it treats the turn as an abnormal exit and can still update reactions or send an error/completion message. Because `turn_participants` is removed here first, that stale notification may also mention the owner fallback instead of the actual participants. In practice, sending `/new` during an active turn can produce a reset acknowledgement followed by a bogus failure/success message from the turn that was just cancelled.
findings.suggestion: Introduce an explicit per-thread reset/cancel marker that `process_turn_events` checks before sending terminal reactions/messages, or otherwise wait for/signal the active turn to shut down cleanly before deleting the session row and clearing thread-local maps. Apply the same fix to the duplicated reset branch in `execute_in_session`.

findings.title: `kill_session` errors are discarded before persistent state is deleted
findings.description: The reset branch uses `let _ = data.sessions.kill_session(...)` and then immediately deletes the DB row and in-memory bookkeeping. That is unsafe because `kill_session` removes the session from the manager before awaiting `child.kill()`, so a failure from `child.kill()` can leave a live subprocess behind after all tracking state has already been erased. At that point the bot has no reliable way to retry cleanup or stop the leaked child later.
findings.suggestion: Do not ignore `kill_session` failures except for an explicit `NotFound`/already-exited case. Log and surface other errors, and only delete the session row after the child was successfully terminated or its exit was otherwise confirmed. Mirror that handling in both reset branches.

summary: The diff does not introduce borrow-checker or mutex-across-await issues, but it does introduce two session-lifecycle regressions around `/new`/`/clear`: stale terminal notifications from cancelled turns and silent cleanup failures when `kill_session` fails.
